cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 52-year-old woman comes in feeling weak and nauseated after several days of barely eating because of a sore mouth and poor appetite. She has lost weight recently and says she has not been keeping up with fluids.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.3 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 31.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (103 + 15) = 17.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently elevated PaCO₂ with renal compensation, resulting in increased bicarbonate and a near-normal pH. This reflects a stable, compensated respiratory acidosis due to long-standing ventilatory limitation.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 29-year-old woman is brought in after several days of vomiting, thirst, and deep rapid breathing. She also mentions a recent flu-like illness with poor oral intake and worsening abdominal discomfort.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 21.1 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 28.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (102 + 14) = 20.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 67-year-old man comes in feeling weak and light-headed after several days of frequent loose stools and poor oral intake. He also reports abdominal cramping and looks mildly dehydrated.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (116 + 13) = 11.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 43-year-old man comes in feeling weak and lightheaded after his diuretic dose was recently increased. He also reports a dry mouth and needing to stand up slowly because of postural dizziness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.7 mmHg (acceptable range 44.7-50.7). The measured PaCO2 is 46.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 33.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.2-35.2). The measured HCO3 is 33.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently elevated PaCO₂ with renal compensation, resulting in increased bicarbonate and a near-normal pH. This reflects a stable, compensated respiratory acidosis due to long-standing ventilatory limitation.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 29-year-old man is brought in after several episodes of vomiting and says he has missed his insulin for the past day. He is thirsty, looks dehydrated, and is breathing faster than usual with mild abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 29.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (92 + 14) = 32.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 32.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 4.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 14 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 10.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: An 18-year-old man was brought in after friends said he had been drinking a home-brewed alcohol at a party and then became increasingly nauseated and confused. He now complains of blurred vision and abdominal pain, and the exact ingredients of the drink are unclear.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 6 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.8 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 17.0 mmHg (acceptable range 15.0-19.0). The measured PaCO2 is 16.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (94 + 6) = 37.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.43, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.0 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.6-22.6). The measured HCO3 is 14.8 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 14.8) = 24.2, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 14.8 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 20.6 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 24.2, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 56-year-old woman is brought in with worsening shortness of breath, a tight chest, and a productive cough over the past day. She is known to smoke and has been increasingly wheezy and drowsy since this morning.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 76.9 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 38.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 36.8-40.8). The measured HCO3 is 34.3 mmol/L. explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62.5 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 66-year-old woman arrives pale and confused with fever, abdominal pain, and cool peripheries. She is tachycardic and looks poorly perfused despite fluids.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10.1 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 5.9 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.1 mmHg (acceptable range 21.1-25.1). The measured PaCO2 is 24.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (100 + 10.1) = 28.9, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 51-year-old man is pale and confused, with cool peripheries and a fast pulse. He has been complaining of vague abdominal pain and feels increasingly unwell over the last few hours.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15.6 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 5.5 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 31.4 mmHg (acceptable range 29.4-33.4). The measured PaCO2 is 30.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (98 + 15.6) = 28.4, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Simple metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24F presents with 3 days of persistent vomiting after a viral illness. She is unable to tolerate oral intake and feels lightheaded on standing. This is her blood gas.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple metabolic alkalosis pattern with elevated bicarbonate and compensatory CO2 retention

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic alkalosis occurs when there is a relative excess of bicarbonate, usually due to loss of hydrogen ions or contraction of extracellular fluid volume. As bicarbonate rises, chloride often falls to maintain electroneutrality. A common cause is loss of gastric acid through excessive vomiting.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 63-year-old woman has become increasingly tired and nauseated over the past few days, with noticeably reduced urine output. Her family say she seems more confused and weak than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 28.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (104 + 13) = 22.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 40-year-old woman is brought in with several days of vomiting, thirst, and worsening abdominal discomfort after a recent flu-like illness. She is breathing deeply and appears markedly dehydrated.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 34.0 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0). The measured PaCO2 is 24.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (97 + 10) = 30.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 36-year-old woman with heavy alcohol use has been shaking, sweaty, and unable to keep food down since stopping drinking two days ago. She says she has barely eaten and now has nausea and upper abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 29.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (95 + 14) = 28.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought in after his parents accidentally rubbed oil of wintergreen on his skin, and his mother is worried he may have also licked some from the bottle. He is sweaty, vomiting, and says his ears are ringing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.2 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.2-23.2). The measured HCO3 is 16.4 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (102 + 16.4) = 20.6, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 16.4 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 21.2 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 20.6, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 22.9 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.6-22.6). The measured HCO3 is 14.3 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (102 + 14.3) = 23.7, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 20.6 mmol/L, but it is 14.3 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 6.3 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 23.7 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 54-year-old woman is brought in by ambulance with worsening shortness of breath and a productive cough over the past few days. She is a smoker and sounds wheezy, with her family saying she is more tired than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 86.5 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 42.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 40.6-44.6). The measured HCO3 is 38.3 mmol/L. This is outside the expected range, so the metabolic compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: An 81-year-old woman has become increasingly tired and nauseated over several days, with reduced urine output. Her family also reports mild confusion and poor appetite.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 35.0 mmHg (acceptable range 33.0-37.0). The measured PaCO2 is 36.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (99 + 18) = 21.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old man is brought in after drinking homemade liquor at a friend’s party, and now has blurred vision, vomiting, and a worsening headache. He seems confused and unsure exactly how much he drank.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.0 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (94 + 13) = 29.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30-year-old man with heavy alcohol use has had two days of vomiting and poor oral intake after a flu-like illness, and now looks dehydrated and shaky.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 30.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (93 + 16) = 24.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 20-year-old man comes in feeling weak and nauseated after several days of barely eating because of a stomach bug. He says he has lost his appetite and has been managing only sips of fluids.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.2 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5). The measured PaCO2 is 33.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (100 + 17) = 21.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought in by his anxious mother after she applied oil of wintergreen to his sore knees and he later became sweaty, nauseated, and started breathing fast. He also mentions a ringing in his ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 29.3 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.9 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.9-23.9). The measured HCO3 is 14.4 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (100 + 14.4) = 25.6, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 14.4 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 21.9 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 25.6, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 73-year-old woman feels dizzy and weak after several days of poor intake and repeated retching. She looks dehydrated and says she has been unable to keep food or fluids down.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 38 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 49.8 mmHg (acceptable range 46.8-52.8). The measured PaCO2 is 51.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 24.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (102 + 12) = 25.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought in after deliberately drinking an unknown amount of antifreeze in a suicide attempt. She is confused, vomiting, and says her vision has become “blurred and strange.”

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 2.6 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 31.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (100 + 15) = 26.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 35-year-old woman with heavy daily alcohol use presents with several days of minimal food intake and ongoing vomiting, and she looks thin and dehydrated. She is shaky, weak, and has some upper abdominal discomfort.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 6 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 17.0 mmHg (acceptable range 15.0-19.0). The measured PaCO2 is 18.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 132 - (90 + 6) = 36.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with repeated vomiting and crampy abdominal pain after several days of thirst and weight loss. She looks dehydrated and is breathing fast and deeply.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (90 + 13) = 33.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 33.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 3.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 13 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 10.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 22 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 41.0 mmHg (acceptable range 39.0-43.0). The measured PaCO2 is 40.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (92 + 22) = 25.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 25.0, so in a pure raised-gap acidosis the bicarbonate would usually be around 11.0 mmol/L. Instead it is 22 mmol/L, preserved by about 11.0 mmol/L. That delta-gap pattern reveals a second metabolic process: a concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought in after a few days of vomiting, thirst, and worsening abdominal discomfort. She also reports a recent flu-like illness with poor oral intake and has been breathing deeply on arrival.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 28.6 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0). The measured PaCO2 is 22.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (99 + 10) = 31.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 37-year-old man is brought in with fever, a productive cough, and increasing breathlessness; he looks pale, confused, and tachypnoeic. His blood pressure is low and he is becoming harder to rouse.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28.3 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.7-23.7). The measured HCO3 is 21.1 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis increases respiratory drive through inflammatory and metabolic signals, leading to hyperventilation and a fall in PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis often occurs early in the disease process before metabolic abnormalities develop.

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 60.4 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 32.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 30.2-34.2). The measured HCO3 is 19.6 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (97 + 19.6) = 25.4, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis should produce a compensatory rise in bicarbonate to about 32.2 mmol/L, but the measured bicarbonate is only 19.6 mmol/L. That shortfall of about 12.6 mmol/L, together with an anion gap of 25.4, supports an additional high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause. A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 50-year-old man feels dizzy on standing and has had a dry mouth and increasing fatigue over the past few days. He recently had his water tablet increased for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 45.2 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 33-year-old man presents with several days of persistent vomiting, poor oral intake, and worsening upper abdominal pain after a recent febrile illness. He looks dehydrated and says he has felt progressively weaker and more lightheaded on standing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

options: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 24 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 44.0 mmHg (acceptable range 42.0-46.0). The measured PaCO2 is 44.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (92 + 24) = 20.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 20.0, so in a pure raised-gap acidosis the bicarbonate would usually be around 16.0 mmol/L. Instead it is 24 mmol/L, preserved by about 8.0 mmol/L. That delta-gap pattern reveals a second metabolic process: a concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24 year old female who recently returned from Bali presents with several days of profuse diarrhoea. She is clinically dry with a mild tachycardia, but is otherwise well.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss leads to a metabolic acidosis, reflected by a low bicarbonate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic acidosis can occur when bicarbonate is lost from the body or when the kidneys are unable to excrete acid effectively. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, which is why the chloride may be elevated. A common cause of this pattern is bicarbonate loss through the gastrointestinal tract, such as with diarrhoea.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: A 31-year-old man is brought in feeling weak and increasingly thirsty after several days of feverish cough and poor oral intake. He has been vomiting intermittently and says his abdominal pain has got worse today.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 23.6 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 26.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (102 + 12) = 27.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.
